title: Design test for diacritic literal appending

content.text:
Discuss with me to write the test "expect State.add cancel existing diacritic will switch to literal for the next charaters" after the test for cancel rules in @src/liblex.zig 

- Prove that the next characters will be appended literally with diacritic processing.
- Use @specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md as source of truth.
- Use existing tests as reference for code pattern, convention and technique.

Come up with the approach, then consult the Oracle for another perspective, but prevent the Oracle from producing any example code. You should not write any code.

content.thinking: I should look at the relevant files to understand what test needs to be written.

result.content:
1: # Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
2: 
3: The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
4: 
5: ## Vietnamese Telex Rules
6: 
7: ### Diacritics
8: 
9: Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
10: Tones have a separate section.
11: 
12: #### Empty
13: 
14: - Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
15: - Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
16:   characters.
17: 
18: #### Circumflex
19: 
20: - Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
21: 
22: #### Breve
23: 
24: - Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
25: 
26: #### Horn
27: 
28: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
29: 
30: #### Stroke
31: 
32: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
33: 
34: #### Apply rules
35: 
36: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
37: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
38:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
39:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
40:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
41: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
42:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
43:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
44:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
45: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
46: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
47: 
48: #### Override rules
49: 
50: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
51:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
52:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
53: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
54: - Keep tone untouched.
55: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
56: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
57: 
58: #### Cancel rules
59: 
60: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
61:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
62: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
63:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
64:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
65:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
66:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
67: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
68: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
69:   remains until the new word.
70:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
71: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
72: 
73: #### Auto-fill rules
74: 
75: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
76:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
77: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
78:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
79:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
80:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
81: - The new input character is appended literally.
82: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
83: 
84: ### Tones
85: 
86: Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
87: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
88: These vowel could take any tone.
89: 
90: #### Level (ngang)
91: 
92: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
93:   corresponding variants.
94: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
95: 
96: #### Rising (sắc)
97: 
98: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
99: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
100:   -> `Ớ`.
101: 
102: #### Falling (huyền)
103: 
104: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
105: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
106:   -> `Ờ`.
107: 
108: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
109: 
110: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
111: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
112:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
113: 
114: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
115: 
116: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
117: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
118:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
119: 
120: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
121: 
122: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
123: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
124:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
125: 
126: #### Apply rules
127: 
128: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
129:   character literally.
130: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
131:   their lower cases.
132: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
133: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
134: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
135:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
136: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
137: 
138: #### Cancel rules
139: 
140: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
141:   character literally.
142: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
143:   their lower cases.
144: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
145: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
146: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
147:   the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
148: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
149:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
150: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
151: 
152: #### Reset rules
153: 
154: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
155:   character literally.
156: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
157: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
158: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
159:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
160: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowel and all of them don't have
161:   any tone (level), append the new character literally.
162: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
163:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
164: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
165: 
166: #### Override rules
167: 
168: - Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
169:   character literally.
170: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
171:   their lower cases.
172: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
173: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
174: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
175:   from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
176:   the word. The override tone is always successful.
177: 
178: #### Scan pseudo-word
179: 
180: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
181:   the tone in the correct position.
182: - The existing characters must not be empty.
183: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
184: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
185:   character to scan, then stop.
186: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
187:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
188: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
189:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
190:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
191: 
192: #### Tone position rules
193: 
194: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
195: 
196: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
197:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
198: - Exceptions:
199:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
200: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
201: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
202:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on `Ơ`.
203:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the listed vowels,
204:     prioritize the first to the last in the list.
205:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
206:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
207:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
208:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
209:     consonants, put on second vowel.
210:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
211: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
212: - Could not put tone for other cases.

content.thinking:
I'm examining the existing test cases to understand how diacritics and literal_index behave during cancellation and override scenarios. There's a test at line 895 that demonstrates cancellation where the previous span retains its base and tone while the diacritic clears, and the new span gets the updated base with an empty diacritic and level tone, with a buffer length of 2.

The key insight from the spec is that after cancellation triggers, subsequent characters that would normally apply Vietnamese transformations—like another 'a' triggering circumflex, 'w' applying breve, or tone markers like 's'—must instead be appended literally without any transformation. So I need to design a test that verifies this behavior: set up a diacritic span, trigger cancellation with a character like 'a', then feed in several characters that would normally transform, and confirm each one creates a new span with just the literal character, empty diacritic, and level tone, while the literal_index remains positioned right after the cancellation point. I need to verify how `buffer_modification_index` behaves when appending literals, and I found that it should be null since no previous span is mutated. For the test, after triggering a cancel action, I'll fire multiple follow-up `state.add` calls to confirm each one appends literally: the buffer length grows by 1, `buffer_modification_index` stays null, `literal_index` remains at position 1, and each new span has the input character as its base with an empty diacritic and level tone. Then I add a second 'a' and verify it stays literal without triggering the circumflex rule, followed by 'w' (which wouldn't apply breve), 's' (which wouldn't apply rising tone), and 'z' (which wouldn't reset tone) — all to confirm that once literal mode persists, no Vietnamese processing rules fire on subsequent characters.

I'll skip the 'z' step since there's nothing to reset anyway. The test structure is a linear narrative with one setup and multiple act-assert pairs, each marked to show the cancel step versus the literal-persistence steps. Now I need to get another perspective from the oracle, so I'm preparing a prompt that explicitly forbids code.

content.text:
## Approach

**Goal of the test**: prove that once a cancel rule fires (per spec section "Cancel rules", lines 58–71), the engine stops applying Vietnamese processing for every subsequent character in the same word — not just the immediate cancel-trigger character (the existing cancel test at [src/liblex.zig#L895-L998](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L895-L998) only proves the cancel-trigger character itself becomes literal).

**What's already proven vs. what's new**:
- Already proven by [src/liblex.zig#L895-L998](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L895-L998): the cancel-trigger character is appended literally, the previous span loses its diacritic, `literal_index = 1`, mode stays `.telex`.
- New property: subsequent characters that would *normally* trigger diacritic apply, breve/horn, stroke, tone apply, or tone reset are all bypassed and appended literally; `literal_index` does not move; previous spans are not mutated; `buffer_modification_index` stays `null` for each literal append.

**Reference example from spec line 70**: `Â` + `aa` → `Aaa` (three spans, all empty diacritic).

**Test shape** (single linear scenario, no `inline for` matrix needed since the existing cancel test already covers the matrix; this new test focuses on the persistence property):

1. **Arrange**: `state.init()`, `state.mode = .telex`, `buffer_effective[0] = Span.init_diacritic_tone('A', .circumflex, .level)` (i.e. `Â`), `buffer_length = 1`.
2. **Act 1 — fire the cancel**: `state.add('a')`. Assert the post-cancel state matches the existing cancel test (buffer_length=2, literal_index=1, sp[0]='A' empty level, sp[1]='a' empty level, mode=.telex). This is a baseline checkpoint, not the property under test.
3. **Act 2 — same-letter would normally trigger circumflex** (`aa` rule): `state.add('a')`. Assert sp[1] is *not* upgraded to `Â`; instead sp[2]='a' empty level. `buffer_length=3`, `literal_index` still `1`, `buffer_modification_index = null`.
4. **Act 3 — `w` would normally apply breve to a preceding `a`**: `state.add('w')`. Assert sp[2] is *not* changed to `ă`; sp[3]='w' empty level. `buffer_length=4`, `literal_index=1`, `buffer_modification_index = null`.
5. **Act 4 — `s` would normally apply rising tone to a vowel**: `state.add('s')`. Assert no vowel in the buffer gains a tone; sp[4]='s' empty level. `buffer_length=5`, `literal_index=1`, `buffer_modification_index = null`.

Each step asserts that all earlier spans are unchanged, so we additionally prove no retroactive mutation.

**Conventions reused from the file**:
- `expectEqual(expected, actual)` argument order.
- Arrange / Act / Assert comment blocks.
- Direct field setup via `state.buffer_effective[i]` and `Span.init_diacritic_tone`.
- Per AGENTS.md: every `State.add` assertion explicitly checks `buffer_modification_index`.

**What is intentionally out of scope** (covered elsewhere or trivially equivalent):
- Backspace re-entering Vietnamese mode (covered by the dedicated backspace tests at [src/liblex.zig#L1004-L1044](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L1004-L1044)).
- Word-boundary reset (separate concern; spec line 69 says "until the new word").
- Cancellation matrix across diacritics/tones (covered by existing cancel test).

Now consulting the Oracle for a second perspective.

input.task:
Review my plan for a new Zig test in the file `src/liblex.zig`. The test name is exactly: "expect State.add cancel existing diacritic will switch to literal for the next charaters" and is to be placed immediately after the existing cancel-rules test at line 895-998 and before the backspace tests at line 1004.

DO NOT WRITE OR PRODUCE ANY CODE, EXAMPLES, SNIPPETS, OR PSEUDO-CODE in your response. Pure prose discussion only. If you think example code would help, describe it in words instead.

Source of truth is the spec at `specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md`, particularly the Cancel rules section (lines 58-71):
- Cancel triggers when user types the character that would have produced the existing diacritic for the same base of the previous span.
- Existing character keeps case and tone but loses the diacritic.
- The new character is literal (keeps case, no tone).
- "Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word."
- Spec example: existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
- Backspacing past the literal point re-enables Vietnamese input (out of scope for this test, covered elsewhere).

What is already proven by the existing cancel test (`expect State.add cancel existing diacritic for valid cases`, lines 895-998): for every (vowel, diacritic, tone) combination plus stroke on D, after one cancel input the previous span loses its diacritic and the new span is literal, with `buffer_length=2`, `literal_index=1`, mode `.telex`, `buffer_modification_index=0`.

What this NEW test must add: prove that the literal mode PERSISTS across MULTIPLE subsequent inputs after the cancel — i.e. characters that would normally trigger any Vietnamese processing (diacritic apply via duplicate letter, breve/horn via `w`, stroke via duplicate `d`, tone apply via `s/f/r/x/j`, tone reset via `z`) are appended literally without mutating any earlier span, with `literal_index` staying put and `buffer_modification_index` becoming `null` for each literal append.

My proposed approach (single linear scenario, no inline for matrix):
1. Arrange: state in .telex mode, buffer_effective[0] = Span.init_diacritic_tone('A', .circumflex, .level) which is `Â`, buffer_length=1.
2. Act `state.add('a')` — fire the cancel. Assert baseline post-cancel state (matches what the existing cancel test asserts: buffer_length=2, literal_index=1, sp[0]='A' empty level, sp[1]='a' empty level, buffer_modification_index=0, mode=.telex).
3. Act `state.add('a')` — would normally trigger circumflex via `aa` rule. Assert sp[1] is NOT upgraded; sp[2]='a' empty level; buffer_length=3, literal_index still 1, buffer_modification_index=null, mode=.telex.
4. Act `state.add('w')` — would normally apply breve to a preceding `a`. Assert sp[2] is NOT changed to `ă`; sp[3]='w' empty level; buffer_length=4, literal_index=1, buffer_modification_index=null.
5. Act `state.add('s')` — would normally apply rising tone to a vowel. Assert no vowel in the buffer gains a tone; sp[4]='s' empty level; buffer_length=5, literal_index=1, buffer_modification_index=null.

Each step also re-asserts that earlier spans (0..i-1) are unchanged, proving no retroactive mutation.

Questions for you:
1. Is this a faithful and minimal test of the spec's "literal input remains until the new word" property?
2. Are there any Vietnamese-processing triggers I should add to the follow-up sequence to make the proof stronger (e.g. `z` for reset, `dd` stroke, `oo` circumflex on a different previous char)?
3. Is choosing a single starting case (`Â` cancelled by `a`) sufficient, or should I parametrize starting cancellations across {circumflex, breve, horn, stroke} to ensure the persistence property holds independently of which cancel kind fired? Justify based on whether the "switch to literal" code path is shared or per-rule in the apparent design.
4. Is there a risk that my Act-2 baseline assertions over-duplicate the existing cancel test, blurring the new test's intent? Should I trim Act-2 assertions to only the post-cancel `literal_index` and let later steps focus purely on persistence?
5. Anything in the spec's wording I might be misinterpreting, especially "until the new word"? The current scenario never crosses a word boundary, which is deliberate, but worth a sanity check.

Reply in prose only. Absolutely no code.
input.context: The project is a Vietnamese Telex input method written in Zig. AGENTS.md mandates: "Every State.add action must decide the value for State.buffer_modification_index because the caller will need it to calculate synthetic backspaces." So every assertion of State.add must include a buffer_modification_index check. Tests use `expectEqual(expected, actual)` argument order and `Arrange / Act / Assert` comment blocks. The Span constructor `Span.init_diacritic_tone(base, diacritic, tone)` is used to seed pre-existing spans directly.

run.result:
1) TL;DR

Your plan is basically right: a single linear scenario is the right shape for this test, because the new thing to prove is persistence after cancel, not cancel correctness itself. I would keep one explicit post-cancel checkpoint, then use follow-up inputs that hit distinct implemented Telex-processing branches; in this codebase today, replacing the tone step with a double-d stroke step gives a stronger and cleaner test.

2) Recommended approach (simple path)

1. Keep the single representative cancel setup.
   - One pre-seeded canceled case is enough for this test’s purpose.
   - Placing it immediately after the cancel matrix test is a good fit.

2. Keep the first post-cancel assertion fairly complete.
   - Do not reduce it to only the literal index.
   - You want one clear checkpoint that proves you successfully entered the “cancel happened, literal starts here” state before testing persistence.
   - That checkpoint should include the modified previous span, the newly appended literal span, buffer length, literal index, mode, and the modification index required by your repo rules.

3. For follow-up inputs, prefer coverage of distinct special-processing families over more cancel variants.
   - Your second input that would normally trigger duplicate-letter diacritic processing is good.
   - Your next input that would normally trigger the w-based diacritic logic is also good.
   - Add a stroke-triggering pair afterward rather than relying on a tone key right now. That gives you coverage of another special branch that is visibly implemented in this file.

4. After each follow-up input, assert the same persistence invariants:
   - buffer length increases by one
   - literal index stays fixed at the cancel point
   - mode remains Telex
   - buffer modification index is null for that add
   - all earlier spans remain unchanged
   - the newly appended span is literal and unprocessed

5. Treat tone-key and reset-key persistence as a separate follow-up test later, unless you are also implementing those branches now.
   - In the current source, those paths do not appear implemented yet.
   - Including them in this test today would blur whether a failure is about literal persistence or simply missing tone/reset behavior.

Effort: S

3) Rationale and trade-offs

Answer to 1: Is this faithful and minimal?

Yes, with one adjustment. The overall structure is faithful to the spec’s “literal input remains until the new word” requirement within a single word, and a single linear scenario is the minimal way to prove persistence over multiple subsequent additions. The only thing I would change is the choice of follow-up triggers: favor ones that clearly exercise implemented Vietnamese-processing branches in the current file.

Answer to 2: Any triggers to add?

Yes: add a stroke-triggering follow-up sequence. That is the highest-value addition.

Why:
- It exercises a different special branch family than duplicate-vowel and w-based diacritic handling.
- It strengthens the claim that literal persistence is not just blocking one vowel-specific path.
- It is more useful than a tone key in the current file, because the tone and reset branches appear to be placeholders right now.

I would not add reset right now unless you are also implementing reset behavior in the same change. If you eventually do add a reset-persistence test, seed the initial span with a non-level tone so a forbidden reset would be observable.

Answer to 3: Is one starting case enough?

Yes, one starting cancellation is sufficient for this test.

Why:
- The existing cancel test already proves that all cancel kinds correctly enter the canceled state for every relevant combination.
- This new test is not about whether cancel works for circumflex, breve, horn, and stroke individually; it is about what happens after literal mode has been entered.
- From the apparent design, the important state transition is “literal index is set.” Once that is true, persistence should follow from the subsequent input handling.

Important nuance:
- The subsequent trigger handling is not fully centralized; several branches appear to check literal mode independently.
- Because of that, extra value comes from covering different follow-up trigger families, not from parameterizing the initial cancel kind again.

So: keep one start case, broaden the follow-up branch coverage.

Answer to 4: Are the baseline assertions too duplicative?

No. Keep them.

This is one of those cases where a little duplication improves test clarity. Without a full post-cancel checkpoint, later failures become ambiguous: you would not know whether the bug is “cancel didn’t establish literal mode correctly” or “literal mode didn’t persist.” Since this new test depends entirely on the state produced by the first add, it is appropriate to assert that state explicitly once.

What you should avoid is re-creating the entire cancel matrix here. One representative post-cancel checkpoint is enough.

Answer to 5: Any spec misread around “until the new word”?

Your reading is sane.

This test should deliberately stay within a single word. In fact, that is the correct scope for this unit test:
- the spec says literal input persists until a new word
- your scenario never crosses a word boundary
- therefore persistence should remain active throughout the whole sequence

Also, in this file, the add function only accepts alphabetic input, so “new word” is not really represented at this level anyway. That makes it even more appropriate to keep this test word-internal and leave re-enabling at boundaries to other tests or higher-level integration behavior.

4) Risks and guardrails

- Biggest risk: using tone or reset keys now.
  - In the current file, those branches look unfinished.
  - If you include them, the test may fail for the wrong reason and stop being a precise persistence test.

- Second risk: choosing follow-ups that do not actually create a realistic mutation opportunity.
  - A good follow-up is one that would definitely mutate an earlier span if literal persistence were broken.
  - That is why duplicate-letter, w-based, and double-d follow-ups are strong.

- Third risk: trimming assertions too aggressively.
  - Because of the AGENTS rule, every add step should explicitly decide the modification index.
  - Keep that check on every act/assert cycle.

- Minor guardrail:
  - Since the test intent is persistence, keep the assertions centered on “append-only, no retroactive mutation, literal index unchanged.”
  - Do not let it turn into another cancel-correctness matrix.

5) When to consider the advanced path

Use a more elaborate test design only if one of these becomes true:
- tone and reset behavior are implemented and you want persistence coverage for those branches too
- word-boundary behavior becomes representable in this layer
- literal handling is refactored in a way that introduces different persistence behavior by trigger family
- you start seeing copy-paste regressions across the per-letter branches

6) Optional advanced path

If you want extra confidence later, split persistence into two focused tests rather than one giant matrix:
- one test for persistence across currently implemented structural triggers
- one test for persistence across tone/reset triggers once those rules exist

That keeps each test crisp and failure messages easier to interpret.

58: #### Cancel rules
59: 
60: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
61:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
62: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
63:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
64:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
65:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
66:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
67: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
68: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
69:   remains until the new word.
70:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
71: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

progress.message:
1) TL;DR

Your plan is basically right: a single linear scenario is the right shape for this test, because the new thing to prove is persistence after cancel, not cancel correctness itself. I would keep one explicit post-cancel checkpoint, then use follow-up inputs that hit distinct implemented Telex-processing branches; in this codebase today, replacing the tone step with a double-d stroke step gives a stronger and cleaner test.

2) Recommended approach (simple path)

1. Keep the single representative cancel setup.
   - One pre-seeded canceled case is enough for this test’s purpose.
   - Placing it immediately after the cancel matrix test is a good fit.

2. Keep the first post-cancel assertion fairly complete.
   - Do not reduce it to only the literal index.
   - You want one clear checkpoint that proves you successfully entered the “cancel happened, literal starts here” state before testing persistence.
   - That checkpoint should include the modified previous span, the newly appended literal span, buffer length, literal index, mode, and the modification index required by your repo rules.

3. For follow-up inputs, prefer coverage of distinct special-processing families over more cancel variants.
   - Your second input that would normally trigger duplicate-letter diacritic processing is good.
   - Your next input that would normally trigger the w-based diacritic logic is also good.
   - Add a stroke-triggering pair afterward rather than relying on a tone key right now. That gives you coverage of another special branch that is visibly implemented in this file.

4. After each follow-up input, assert the same persistence invariants:
   - buffer length increases by one
   - literal index stays fixed at the cancel point
   - mode remains Telex
   - buffer modification index is null for that add
   - all earlier spans remain unchanged
   - the newly appended span is literal and unprocessed

5. Treat tone-key and reset-key persistence as a separate follow-up test later, unless you are also implementing those branches now.
   - In the current source, those paths do not appear implemented yet.
   - Including them in this test today would blur whether a failure is about literal persistence or simply missing tone/reset behavior.

Effort: S

3) Rationale and trade-offs

Answer to 1: Is this faithful and minimal?

Yes, with one adjustment. The overall structure is faithful to the spec’s “literal input remains until the new word” requirement within a single word, and a single linear scenario is the minimal way to prove persistence over multiple subsequent additions. The only thing I would change is the choice of follow-up triggers: favor ones that clearly exercise implemented Vietnamese-processing branches in the current file.

Answer to 2: Any triggers to add?

Yes: add a stroke-triggering follow-up sequence. That is the highest-value addition.

Why:
- It exercises a different special branch family than duplicate-vowel and w-based diacritic handling.
- It strengthens the claim that literal persistence is not just blocking one vowel-specific path.
- It is more useful than a tone key in the current file, because the tone and reset branches appear to be placeholders right now.

I would not add reset right now unless you are also implementing reset behavior in the same change. If you eventually do add a reset-persistence test, seed the initial span with a non-level tone so a forbidden reset would be observable.

Answer to 3: Is one starting case enough?

Yes, one starting cancellation is sufficient for this test.

Why:
- The existing cancel test already proves that all cancel kinds correctly enter the canceled state for every relevant combination.
- This new test is not about whether cancel works for circumflex, breve, horn, and stroke individually; it is about what happens after literal mode has been entered.
- From the apparent design, the important state transition is “literal index is set.” Once that is true, persistence should follow from the subsequent input handling.

Important nuance:
- The subsequent trigger handling is not fully centralized; several branches appear to check literal mode independently.
- Because of that, extra value comes from covering different follow-up trigger families, not from parameterizing the initial cancel kind again.

So: keep one start case, broaden the follow-up branch coverage.

Answer to 4: Are the baseline assertions too duplicative?

No. Keep them.

This is one of those cases where a little duplication improves test clarity. Without a full post-cancel checkpoint, later failures become ambiguous: you would not know whether the bug is “cancel didn’t establish literal mode correctly” or “literal mode didn’t persist.” Since this new test depends entirely on the state produced by the first add, it is appropriate to assert that state explicitly once.

What you should avoid is re-creating the entire cancel matrix here. One representative post-cancel checkpoint is enough.

Answer to 5: Any spec misread around “until the new word”?

Your reading is sane.

This test should deliberately stay within a single word. In fact, that is the correct scope for this unit test:
- the spec says literal input persists until a new word
- your scenario never crosses a word boundary
- therefore persistence should remain active throughout the whole sequence

Also, in this file, the add function only accepts alphabetic input, so “new word” is not really represented at this level anyway. That makes it even more appropriate to keep this test word-internal and leave re-enabling at boundaries to other tests or higher-level integration behavior.

4) Risks and guardrails

- Biggest risk: using tone or reset keys now.
  - In the current file, those branches look unfinished.
  - If you include them, the test may fail for the wrong reason and stop being a precise persistence test.

- Second risk: choosing follow-ups that do not actually create a realistic mutation opportunity.
  - A good follow-up is one that would definitely mutate an earlier span if literal persistence were broken.
  - That is why duplicate-letter, w-based, and double-d follow-ups are strong.

- Third risk: trimming assertions too aggressively.
  - Because of the AGENTS rule, every add step should explicitly decide the modification index.
  - Keep that check on every act/assert cycle.

- Minor guardrail:
  - Since the test intent is persistence, keep the assertions centered on “append-only, no retroactive mutation, literal index unchanged.”
  - Do not let it turn into another cancel-correctness matrix.

5) When to consider the advanced path

Use a more elaborate test design only if one of these becomes true:
- tone and reset behavior are implemented and you want persistence coverage for those branches too
- word-boundary behavior becomes representable in this layer
- literal handling is refactored in a way that introduces different persistence behavior by trigger family
- you start seeing copy-paste regressions across the per-letter branches

6) Optional advanced path

If you want extra confidence later, split persistence into two focused tests rather than one giant matrix:
- one test for persistence across currently implemented structural triggers
- one test for persistence across tone/reset triggers once those rules exist

That keeps each test crisp and failure messages easier to interpret.

content.text:
## Reconciled approach

Oracle's review converges with my plan, with two refinements:

**Refinement A — replace the tone-trigger step with a stroke-trigger step.** Oracle observes that tone/reset branches in `liblex.zig` look unfinished today; using `s` would risk the test failing for the wrong reason. A duplicate-`d` step exercises a different special branch family (stroke) than `aa` (circumflex via dup) and `w` (breve/horn), and is fully implemented today. So the follow-up sequence becomes: `a`, `w`, `d`, `d` (the second `d` being the one that would normally apply stroke to the first).

**Refinement B — keep the post-cancel checkpoint complete, not trimmed.** A full checkpoint after the cancel disambiguates future failures ("did cancel fail?" vs. "did persistence fail?"). Acceptable duplication with the existing matrix test; the new test still avoids re-running the matrix.

**Confirmed unchanged:**
- One starting case (`Â` cancelled by `a`) is sufficient — persistence is gated on `literal_index != null`, not on which cancel kind set it.
- Single linear scenario, no `inline for`.
- Word-boundary re-enable stays out of scope (only alphabetic input flows through `State.add`; backspace tests cover re-entry).
- Every `state.add` assertion includes `buffer_modification_index` per AGENTS.md.
- Each persistence step also re-asserts that all earlier spans are unchanged.

**Final test outline:**

1. Arrange: `state.init()`, `state.mode = .telex`, `buffer_effective[0] = Span.init_diacritic_tone('A', .circumflex, .level)`, `buffer_length = 1`.
2. Act `state.add('a')` (cancel). Full checkpoint: `buffer_length=2`, `literal_index=1`, `buffer_modification_index=0`, mode `.telex`, sp[0]=`A` empty level, sp[1]=`a` empty level.
3. Act `state.add('a')` (would-be circumflex via duplicate). Assert sp[1] unchanged, sp[2]=`a` empty level, `buffer_length=3`, `literal_index=1`, `buffer_modification_index=null`.
4. Act `state.add('w')` (would-be breve). Assert sp[2] unchanged, sp[3]=`w` empty level, `buffer_length=4`, `literal_index=1`, `buffer_modification_index=null`.
5. Act `state.add('d')` (literal seed for stroke). Assert sp[4]=`d` empty level, `buffer_length=5`, `literal_index=1`, `buffer_modification_index=null`.
6. Act `state.add('d')` (would-be stroke via duplicate `d`). Assert sp[4] unchanged (still plain `d`, not `Đ`), sp[5]=`d` empty level, `buffer_length=6`, `literal_index=1`, `buffer_modification_index=null`.

Tone/reset persistence deferred to a separate test once those branches are implemented.

Awaiting your go-ahead before writing the test.
Yes, I agree with your plan. Write the test.

content.text:
All 19 tests pass, including the new test #15 "expect State.add cancel existing diacritic will switch to literal for the next charaters".

Summary:
- Added test at [src/liblex.zig#L1000](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L1000) following the agreed plan: one starting case (`Â` cancelled by `a`), then four follow-up inputs (`a`, `w`, `d`, `d`) covering circumflex-via-duplicate, breve, and stroke-via-duplicate trigger families.
- Each step asserts `buffer_length`, `buffer_modification_index`, `literal_index`, `mode`, plus full state of every span (proves no retroactive mutation).
- Tone/reset persistence deliberately deferred until those branches are implemented.
